title: Securing Public Clients with PKCE
description:
## Auth Code Flow for a Mobile App

You are building a **native mobile app**. It is a public client and **cannot keep a client secret** confidential. You still want the security of the Authorization Code flow and protection against someone intercepting the returned authorization code.

Which approach should you use?

options.text: Authorization Code flow secured with a **client secret** shipped inside the app binary.
The **implicit flow**, returning tokens directly in the redirect.
The **Client Credentials** grant, since the app can't hold a secret.

explanation: **PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange)** is designed exactly for public clients. The app generates a random **`code_verifier`**, sends its hash as the **`code_challenge`** when starting the flow, and later presents the original verifier when exchanging the code. An attacker who intercepts the authorization code cannot redeem it without the verifier — so PKCE secures the code flow **without any client secret**. Option **b** is unsafe: a secret in a distributed binary can be extracted, so it provides no real protection. Option **c** (implicit flow) is **deprecated** and exposes tokens in the redirect URL. Option **d** is wrong because Client Credentials is for **machine-to-machine** access with no user — it can't log a human in. Today PKCE is recommended for *all* clients, but it is **essential** for public ones.

hints.title: Bind the request to the redeemer
hints.body: PKCE ties the authorization request to the party that will redeem the code, so a stolen code is useless on its own.
